summary: This post introduces Elinor Ostrom's Nobel Prize-winning work on common pool resource management as a counterpoint to pessimistic game theory models like the tragedy of the commons. Ostrom demonstrated that communities can sustainably manage shared resources without market or state intervention when certain conditions are met: defined boundaries, localized rules, monitoring, and high mutual trust among participants. The author connects this framework to open source sustainability, arguing that the production of digital public goods has rivalrous properties similar to physical commons. Rather than seeking a single solution to open source funding, Ostrom's work suggests cataloging behavioral differences between communities to find emergent patterns. The post concludes that high-context, self-organized solutions are more likely to succeed than low-context external interventions.
key_points: Ostrom's research shows commons can be sustainably managed without market or state intervention when communities have defined boundaries, localized rules, and accountability mechanisms
Sustainable commons management requires high context among participants - most failures trace back to context collapse
Communities need high mutual trust and long-term interest (low discount rate) to self-regulate effectively
Scaling doesn't mean cramming more people into one community, but acknowledging boundaries and governing at multiple levels (polycentric approach)
High-context solutions like funding specific contributors are more likely to succeed than low-context solutions like bounties to strangers
money_quotes: Sustainably managing the commons requires a high degree of context among participants. Most failure outcomes can be traced back to context collapse.
Managing at scale doesn't mean stuffing more and more people into the same community, but acknowledging boundaries and working together to govern at multiple levels.
Nobody wants to be told the answer is complicated, whereas the other models are simple, even if they're pessimistic and reductive.
Perhaps the goal is to understand and catalog these behavioral differences, so that commonalities emerge between them.

Maybe now is the time to cast aside the market and the state as our saviors, and instead consider an alternative: one that recognizes the ability of people to organize and design solutions for themselves.
When individuals have developed shared norms and patterns of reciprocity, they possess social capital with which they can build institutional arrangements for resolving CPR dilemmas.
key_insight: Communities can sustainably manage shared resources through high-context self-organization rather than external intervention, but this requires defined boundaries, mutual trust, and long-term commitment from participants.
